agent_output:
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Payment function has no idempotency guard, no atomicity between charge and DB update, and no order validation — will double-charge customers and lose payment records in production."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Charge succeeds but DB update fails = money taken, order not marked paid",
      "risk": "If `db.orders.update` throws (network blip, DB down, constraint violation), the customer is charged via the payment gateway but the order remains unpaid in the database. No compensation (refund/retry) exists. Support has no record the charge happened. Retrying the function charges the customer again.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "processPayment — lines between paymentGateway.charge() and db.orders.update()",
      "reproduction": "1. Call processPayment with valid inputs. 2. Simulate DB failure after charge (e.g., kill DB connection mid-flight). 3. Observe: gateway has the charge, orders table still shows 'unpaid'. 4. Customer retries → double charge.",
      "fix": "Wrap in a saga pattern: (1) persist a payment intent record BEFORE calling the gateway, (2) call the gateway, (3) update order status. On failure after charge, schedule an async refund or reconciliation job. Alternatively, use the gateway's idempotency key so retries are safe.",
      "time_to_fix": "2-4 hours",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Payment reliability, data consistency, no double-charges"
    },
    {
      "title": "No idempotency — retries and duplicate requests double-charge customers",
      "risk": "No idempotency key is passed to paymentGateway.charge(). If the client retries (timeout, network error, user double-click), or if a queue redelivers this message, a new charge is created every time. Most payment gateways (Stripe, Braintree, Adyen) support idempotency keys specifically to prevent this — not using one is a critical oversight.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "processPayment — paymentGateway.charge(cardToken, amount)",
      "reproduction": "1. Client sends POST /pay for order 123. 2. Response times out at the HTTP layer (charge succeeded on gateway). 3. Client retries identical request. 4. Second charge created on the gateway. Customer charged twice.",
      "fix": "Generate a deterministic idempotency key from orderId (e.g., `payment_${orderId}`) and pass it to the gateway: `paymentGateway.charge(cardToken, amount, { idempotencyKey: orderId })`. Also add a status check at the top: if order.status === 'paid', return early.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Exactly-once payment processing"
    },
    {
      "title": "No order validation — can pay wrong amount, pay for non-existent or already-paid orders",
      "risk": "The function fetches the order but never uses it. Three bugs: (1) `order` could be null (deleted/invalid orderId) — subsequent code proceeds, charges the card, then the update silently fails or throws. (2) The `amount` parameter is trusted from the caller, never validated against `order.amount` — an attacker or bug can charge $0.01 for a $1000 order. (3) No check for `order.status === 'paid'` — already-completed orders can be re-charged.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "processPayment — after db.orders.findById(orderId)",
      "reproduction": "1. Call processPayment('nonexistent-id', 100, token) → charges card, update throws. 2. Call processPayment(orderId, 0.01, token) for a $500 order → charges 1 cent, marks as paid. 3. Call processPayment for an already-paid order → charges customer again.",
      "fix": "Add validation immediately after the fetch: `if (!order) throw new PaymentError('Order not found'); if (order.status === 'paid') return { success: true, orderId }; if (amount !== order.totalAmount) throw new PaymentError('Amount mismatch');`",
      "time_to_fix": "20 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Payment correctness, revenue integrity, security"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "No error handling or logging",
      "description": "The function has zero try/catch, no logging of charge attempts or failures, and no structured error return. A gateway timeout surfaces as an unhandled promise rejection with no audit trail. Payment operations require an audit log for compliance and dispute resolution.",
      "location": "processPayment — entire function body"
    },
    {
      "title": "Race condition on concurrent calls for same order",
      "description": "Two concurrent calls for the same orderId will both pass the (currently missing) status check, both call the gateway, and both charge the customer. Needs a distributed lock or SELECT FOR UPDATE on the order row before proceeding.",
      "location": "processPayment — no locking mechanism"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Return a structured error type (not just { success: true }) so callers can distinguish gateway declines, validation errors, and infrastructure failures",
    "Add structured logging with correlation ID for every payment attempt — charge attempt, charge result, DB update result — for audit and dispute resolution",
    "Consider making this function transactional: wrap DB reads/writes in a transaction and use SELECT FOR UPDATE on the order row to prevent concurrent double-charges"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Function will silently double-charge customers on any retry, lose payment records on DB failures, and allow amount manipulation. These are not edge cases — they are normal production scenarios (network timeouts, user double-clicks).",
    "rollback_capability": "N/A — standalone function review, not a deployment.",
    "backward_compatibility": "N/A — new function.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "Zero. No logging, no metrics, no alerting. Payment failures are invisible.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": false,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": []
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Do not deploy. Fix idempotency, atomicity, and validation before shipping."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 97,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Double-charge risk from missing idempotency is certain — this is how every payment gateway documents the problem",
      "Atomicity gap between charge and DB update is a textbook partial-failure scenario",
      "Missing order validation is visible in the code — order is fetched but never read"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Cannot verify whether the caller or middleware provides any of these protections (idempotency, validation) at a higher layer — reviewing this function in isolation"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "paymentGateway.charge() creates a real charge with no built-in deduplication",
      "No upstream middleware provides idempotency or amount validation",
      "db.orders.update does not implicitly check order status"
    ]
  }
}

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this payment processing function for critical issues.

Classification: `none` — all fixes are code-level (transactions, idempotency, error handling).

Key hypotheses:
1. No idempotency — double charge risk
2. No transaction/atomicity — charge succeeds but DB update fails = paid but not recorded
3. No validation of amount vs order amount — charge arbitrary amounts
4. No error handling — partial failure leaves inconsistent state
5. No order status check — can pay for already-paid orders
